message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The value of frequent, rapid testing to reduce community transmission of SARS-CoV-2 is poorly understood.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To define performance standards and predict the clinical, epidemiological, and economic outcomes of nationwide, home-based, antigen testing.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A simple compartmental epidemic model estimated viral transmission, clinical history, and resource use, with and without testing.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Data Sources</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Parameter values and ranges informed by Centers for Disease Control guidance and published literature.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Target Population</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>United States population.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Time Horizon</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>60 days.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Perspective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Societal.Costs include: testing, inpatient care, and lost workdays.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Intervention</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Home-based SARS-CoV-2 antigen testing.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Outcome Measures</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Cumulative infections and deaths, numbers isolated and/or hospitalized, and total costs.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results of Base-Case Analysis</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Without a testing intervention, the model anticipates 15 million infections, 125,000 deaths, and $10.4 billion in costs ($6.5 billion inpatient; $3.9 billion lost productivity) over a 60-day horizon. Weekly availability of testing may avert 4 million infections and 19,000 deaths, raising costs by $21.5 billion. Lower inpatient outlays ($5.9 billion) would partially offset additional testing expenditures ($12.0 billion) and workdays lost ($13.9 billion), yielding incremental costs per infection (death) averted of $5,400 ($1,100,000).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results of Sensitivity Analysis</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Outcome estimates vary widely under different behavioral assumptions and testing frequencies. However, key findings persist across all scenarios: large reductions in infections, mortality, and hospitalizations; and costs per death averted roughly an order of magnitude lower than commonly accepted willingness-to-pay values per statistical life saved ($5-17 million).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Limitations</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Analysis restricted to at-home testing and limited by uncertainties about test performance.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>High-frequency home testing for SARS-CoV-2 using an inexpensive, imperfect test could contribute to pandemic control at justifiable cost and warrants consideration as part of a national containment strategy.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Primary Funding Sources</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Dr. Paltiel was supported by grant R37DA015612 from the National Institute on Drug Abuse of the National Institutes of Health.</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>Dr. Sax was supported by grant R01AI042006 from the National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases of the National Institutes of Health.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Clinical and Economic Impact of Widespread Rapid Testing to Decrease SARS-CoV-2 Transmission
